does: This paper investigates how to steer the internal representations of a Transformer-based music generation model to control specific musical attributes without retraining. The authors use the Difference-in-Means methodology to identify latent directions in the residual stream corresponding to Pitch and Duration in the Multitrack Music Transformer. They validate the Linear Representation Hypothesis, showing strong correlations between steering magnitude and attribute shifts. To handle interference when steering multiple attributes simultaneously, they introduce a Dual Steering framework that applies Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization to geometrically decouple the steering vectors. Experimental results demonstrate that this orthogonal decomposition reduces feature entanglement and signal degradation compared to naive vector addition approaches, enabling independent control of multiple musical attributes at inference time.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research into mechanistic interpretability of neural networks and representation steering. It extends prior work on activation steering and the Linear Representation Hypothesis, which has been explored in language models and vision transformers, to the domain of symbolic music generation. The paper draws on interpretability techniques like Difference-in-Means probing that have been used to isolate latent concepts in transformer architectures. The approach also connects to research on multi-attribute control in generative models and responds to the challenge of feature entanglement identified in multi-dimensional steering tasks. The use of orthogonalization for decoupling follows established geometric approaches in linear algebra and builds on methods for controlling multiple conditions in neural generation without retraining.
enables: This framework opens pathways for fine-grained, interpretable control over symbolic music generation without model retraining, which could accelerate interactive music creation tools and enable musicians to precisely shape generated compositions in real time. The geometric decoupling approach via orthogonalization could generalize to other multi-attribute steering problems in transformer models beyond music, including text generation and other sequential domains. By demonstrating that activation steering can handle complex entangled features, this work suggests that similar mechanistic interpretability techniques could unlock control over additional musical attributes such as tempo, dynamics, or instrumentation. The interpretable nature of the approach also contributes to broader efforts in understanding and safely controlling large generative models, potentially informing better practices for auditable and controllable AI systems in creative and safety-critical domains.
